entry.title: Prompt-Driven Development: Building Full-Stack SAP CAP Apps from User Stories
entry.content:
Introduction

  • What if you could translate a business requirement into a functional, full-stack application with a single prompt?
  • How much faster could you deliver value if the foundational code and project scaffolding were generated for you in seconds?

This is the new reality powered by SAP Build Code and its integrated AI copilot, Joule.

As a turn-key environment on the SAP Business Technology Platform, it unifies the entire development lifecycle, from coding to deployment.

This post demonstrates how to build a full-stack SAP CAP application directly from a user story, showcasing how

generative AI is fundamentally reshaping the developer experience.

 

Business Requirement

Build an SAP CAP application based on the requirements in the user story below:

User Story

As a contract administrator, I want to create and manage contracts and ‘parties involved’ information in the system,

so that I can effectively track and handle legal agreements and ‘parties involved’ interactions.

Contracts can cover a vast range of agreements, such as sales contracts, service agreements and employment contracts.

Party involved: A ‘party involved’ is an individual, organization, or entity that purchases goods, products, or services from another party,

typically a business or seller.

Common attributes of a contract might include:

  1. Contract ID: A unique identifier for the contract.
  2. Party involved Identification Number: A unique identification number for the party involved.
  3. Contract Type: The type of contract (e.g. sales, service, employment).
  4. Start Date: The date on which the contract becomes valid.
  5. End Date: The date on which the contract expires (if applicable).
  6. Status: The current status of the contract (draft, active, expired, terminated, etc.)

A typical ‘party involved’ has the following attributes:

  1. Party involved Identification Number
  2. Party involved Name: It refers to the given name of a person or entity, or a label by which they are addressed or identified.
  3. Party involved Address: It refers to a physical location where an individual, business, or entity is situated or can be reached.
  4. Party involved Contact Information

Acceptance Criteria

Scenario 1: List All Contracts

Given I am logged into the contract management system, when I launch the SAP Fiori application to maintain contracts,

then I should be able to view the list of all the contracts in a list without pressing the GO button.

The list of all the contracts should have : Contract ID, Party involved Identification Number, Contract Type and Start Date. 

The list of filters should include Contract Type, Contract Status and Start Date.

Scenario 2: View Contract Details 

Given I am logged into the contract management system, when I select a specific contract from the list of SAP Fiori application,

then I should be able to view the contract details and parties involved information.

 

Joule's Generative AI-based SAP CAP App Development

Prerequisites

  • Get a Free Account on SAP BTP Trial
  • Setup SAP Build Code

1. Create a New Project using SAP Build Lobby

Create a Project

 

Click the Application Tile

Full-Stack

Full-Stack Node.JS

Contract Management

Contract Management

Project Created

2. Build a Full-Stack CAP Application via Prompt

Click the created project to open it in SAP Business Application Studio

Open Joule From the Activity Bar and  select the ’/cap-gen-app` slash command

Generate a CAP Application

Prompt the user story description(refer to Business Requirement section above) to Joule and click send.

Joule tackles complex tasks by breaking them into steps, each with a suggested prompt.

Diffrerent Steps

To simplify the process and provide full context, replace the content of each step with the complete user story prompt.

Then, save and run to generate each step's artifacts.

Accept the Code.

Accept the Code

Generated Artifacts

3. Add UI to the Application

Create a new Fiori elements Application.

Fiori Elements Application

 

3. Preview the Application

Click run to preview the Application.

Run the Application

The application’s preview is displayed.

Application Preview

Launch the contract management app.

Contract Management

Select a contract from the list to view its details.

Select Contract

 

Contract details

Conclusion

The journey from user story to a functional, full-stack application—once a multi-day endeavor—is now accomplished in minutes.

With SAP Build Code and its AI copilot, Joule, this dramatic acceleration signals a fundamental paradigm shift.

The developer's role is being elevated from the meticulous craft of writing code to the strategic art of guiding its creation.

Joule operates as the quintessential collaborator, executing the foundational groundwork to liberate developers to focus

on high-value business logic and innovation.

SAP Build Code is therefore more than a tool; it's the dawn of an era where the speed of technology finally

matches the ambition of business.

 

Further Information

SAP Build Code

#SAPBuildCode #Joule #SAPBTP

 

______________________________________________________________________________________________

 

 

Best Regards,
Anuradha Dhingan

entry.title: SAP Fiori tools 2601 update – Mobile Device Preview, Building Blocks, and AI-Driven Productivity
entry.content:
With the latest SAP Fiori tools update 2601, we continue our focus on improving developer productivity, openness, and real-world usability. This release brings meaningful enhancements across previewing applications, managing system connections, building SAP Fiori elements–based UIs, and accelerating development with AI-powered tooling. At the same time, we’ve continued to refine the fundamentals in security, modelling, and extensibility.

Preview SAP Fiori Apps on Real Mobile Devices

When developing SAP Fiori applications, simulating a mobile experience in a desktop browser often isn’t enough to fully validate real-world behavior. Touch interactions, performance characteristics, and device-specific rendering can differ noticeably on physical phones and tablets.

To address this, SAP Fiori tools now supports previewing applications directly on real mobile devices when using @sap/ux-ui5-tooling version 1.19.1 or higher. By creating a new run configuration and enabling Remote Access, developers can expose the local preview. When the run configuration is started, the terminal provides connection details, including a URL and a QR code, which can be opened directly on a phone or tablet to access the running app.

This makes it significantly easier to validate layouts, navigation, and interactions exactly as end users will experience them without leaving the development environment.

Showcase for previewing a SAP Fiori elements app on iPhone

A New Connection Manager for SAP Systems in VS Code

Managing connections to remote SAP systems is a foundational task when working with SAP Fiori tools. With this release, we introduce the Connection Manager for SAP Systems as a dedicated Visual Studio Code extension. This new extension succeeds the previously integrated saved systems panel from the Application Modeler and provides a focused, extensible solution for handling system connections in Visual Studio Code.

The Connection Manager allows developers to create, store, test, and edit connection information that can be reused by the SAP Fiori generator as well as by preview features in SAP Fiori tools. Storing passwords is optional, and if stored, they are protected by the security policies of your operating system's credential manager.

By making this capability standalone and open source, it is now accessible to other extensions such as the Service Catalog extension, and open for community contributions.

Connection Manager

Expanded Support for SAP Fiori Elements Building Blocks

SAP Fiori tools continues to enhance the support for the building blocks which shape the framework of SAP Fiori elements for OData V4 and its flexibility you can use. It makes it easier to create and adapt applications using a structured, metadata-driven approach while keeping flexibility in design. In this update, the Page Editor gains additional capabilities that improve developer convenience when working with custom pages and sections.

Developers can now add more building block types to their apps with the Page Editor, including rich text editors within custom sections, custom columns for table building blocks, and the page building block itself. Managing custom pages is also more intuitive, as building blocks can now be deleted directly from the editor. To support experimentation and iteration, undo and redo actions are now available when working on custom pages.

Together, these enhancements reduce friction when adapting applications and allow developers to work more confidently and efficiently with SAP Fiori elements–based UIs.

Rich Text Editor building block support in Page Editor

Enhancements Across the AI-Toolchain

AI capabilities continue to evolve across SAP Fiori tools, with a strong focus on usability and enabling deeper end-to-end changes from within the tooling.

The Project Accelerator can now be launched directly from the SAP Fiori generator, streamlining the initial project creation flow. The previously required staging step has been removed, allowing new projects to be created directly in the selected workspace folder. For projects created using the Project Accelerator, the Application Modeler now offers advanced editing capabilities, such as creating new fields and properties end-to-end, updating the data model, exposing the service, and consuming the changes in the UI in one coherent flow.

AI support has also been extended to mock data generation. Developers can generate realistic mock data using AI directly in the Application Modeler, and this capability is available for all applications whenever the Project Accelerator is enabled.

In parallel, the MCP server for SAP Fiori continues to expand. The server now exposes @sap-ux/create commands as CLI functionality, enabling reliable execution in MCP-supported workflows. App generation has been extended to support CAP Java projects, and a new “search_docs” tool allows the LLM (Large Language Model) to query SAP Fiori elements documentation, annotations, SAP Fiori tools documentation, and SAP Fiori Development Portal resources to quickly find relevant guidance and code snippets.

Continuous Improvements and Refinements

Beyond the headline features, this release includes a range of continuous improvements that strengthen the overall development experience. Newly created CAP applications now use NPM workspaces by default, establishing best practices out of the box and enabling the use of @sap/ux-ui5-tooling middlewares when previewing CAP-based applications.

The Application Modeler has been enhanced with additional SAP Fiori elements for OData V4 features, including support for hidden draft mode, improved configuration options for table create dialogs, and create support for custom fields in form sections. These additions give developers more control over application behavior without leaving the modeling environment.

Security and user management have also been strengthened. The ABAP Cloud connection type in the Connection Manager has been consolidated around re-entrance ticket authentication, moving away from service key–based authentication. This eliminates long-lived shared credentials and ties system access directly to the signed-in user, improving both security and maintainability.

Looking Ahead

We are already working on the next set of enhancements for SAP Fiori tools. Planned improvements include a rework and enhancement of OPA5 test generation, with added support for value help services and mock data generation. Project validation will be extended with new domain-specific ESLint rule sets, visualized directly in the Application Modeler, alongside support for ESLint 9.

Support for additional SAP Fiori elements building blocks, such as form and field, is also on the roadmap. On the AI side, we continue to improve the robustness and usefulness of responses from the MCP server, while the Project Accelerator will be extended to support RAP service creation.

As always, we look forward to your feedback and contributions as we continue to evolve SAP Fiori tools together with the community.

entry.title: How to use the new Google IDE Antigravity with SAP (and CAP)
entry.content:
We hear that with the new SAP ABAP Cloud on Visual Studio Code, more and more developers will move to the “new” IDE.
We already know that Business Application Studio is a cousin of Visual Studio Code.
Do you know who another cousin is?

Spoiler
Google Antigravity!

In this brief blog post, I will present what it is, why you should use it, how to install all components, how to use it with SAP, and my final thoughts after an intensive weekend of use.

This guide focuses on Windows and Node.js installation (no Java). ⚠️
My personal impressions are from an Apple user perspective. 🤙

What is Google Antigravity?

Google Antigravity is a new IDE forked from Visual Studio Code.
I started using it based on a colleague’s suggestion, mainly because:

  • Advanced agent-based development model
  • Google as a strong and reliable engineering brand
  • Support for running multiple agents in parallel
  • Unique planning and “vibe coding” experience
  • Temporary free access to Gemini Pro and Opus models (only for this you should try it!)

Why not give it a try?

Download Antigravity

Google Antigravity Download

If you have never used Visual Studio Code before

The following steps help you get started if you are completely new to Visual Studio Code and Antigravity.

 

1. Install Node.js

Download the latest Node.js version and complete the installation:

Node.js — Download Node.js®

 

2. Install CDS Tools for CAP Development

Install the CAP development kit globally:

npm i -g @sap/cds-dk

Note: the -g parameter installs the tool globally and only needs to be executed once.

3. Install Cloud Foundry CLI

The CF CLI is required for Cloud Foundry operations such as application deployment and service management.

You can download it from the SAP Development Tools page (Cloud tab):

SAP Development Tools

 

Verify the installation:

cf -v

If Antigravity was already open during installation, restart it.

Install the Multi-Target Application plugin:

cf install-plugin multiapps

5. Install Chocolatey and MTA Build Tool (MBT)

Chocolatey is optional. I use it to install GNU Make, which is required by the MTA Build Tool during the build process.

Chocolatey installation guide:

Installing Chocolatey

Install the MTA Build Tool:

npm install -g mbt

Verify the installation:

mbt -v

Configure Plugins and Marketplace for SAP in Antigravity

Antigravity is based on an open-source ecosystem and does not have direct access to all official VS Code plugins. This requires a small configuration change.

From my perspective, the most important plugins are:

  • SAP Fiori Tools
  • CDS Tools
  • CAP Tools
  • SAPUI5 Extension
  • UI5 XML Support
  • Remote Access for SAP Business Application Studio

Go to File > Preferences > Antigravity Settings

 

Set the following values:

 

Marketplace Item URL:
https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items

Marketplace Gallery URL:
https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/_apis/public/gallery

Please restart Antigravity after this change ⚠️

Install all your favorite plugins:

 

Configure MCP Server

At this point, I recommend configuring the MCP server with the Antigravity AI chat. Here I only describe the CAP MCP configuration, but if you want more info, this is the original post with the most important MCP servers: SAP Build introduces new MCP Servers to enable age... - SAP Community.

In the agent view, select the three dots (…) and choose MCP Servers


, then Manage MCP Servers,


and finally View raw configuration.

Paste the following configuration:

{
  "servers": {
    "cds-mcp": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@cap-js/mcp-server"],
      "env": {},
      "type": "stdio"
    }
  },
  "inputs": []
}


Restart Antigravity to complete the setup. ⚠️

To learn more about MCP servers, refer to: MCP – A Comprehensive Guide

Final Conclusion after Intensive Use

Antigravity is still a young product. I had to restart it multiple times during my work, and I consumed the Gemini Pro and Opus credits fairly quickly.

Visual Studio Code still feels more stable, and SAP’s tooling is clearly more mature there. However, switching between the two is extremely easy, which makes experimentation low risk.

I did not fully experience the benefits of multi-model usage yet—this may be due to configuration or limited testing time. Still, I recommend exploring it and trying it out during your vibe coding sessions.

Let me know how your experience goes and what your impression is of this new IDE.
Pro or Cons?

entry.title: Using Cline with SAP AI Core in VS Code: A Complete Setup Guide
entry.content:
As SAP developers, we spend our days juggling multiple tools such as code editors, documentation pages, AI Launchpad, and API testing platforms. With information scattered across different places, a significant amount of time is lost switching between tabs instead of focusing on actual development. Now imagine if SAP’s GenAI capabilities were available right where you work, ready to support you the moment you need them.

That is exactly what Cline integrated with SAP AI Core delivers. Cline works like an AI coding partner inside your development environment, and when connected to SAP AI Core, it brings SAP’s GenAI Hub models directly into your workflow. There are no API calls to write and no dashboards to open. You simply type a prompt and receive instant, SAP-aware assistance for CAP, UI5, CDS, integrations, and more.

Setting it up is simple. Install two extensions and connect your SAP AI Core service key. In just a few minutes, your development environment transforms into an AI-enhanced workspace where you can generate code, debug faster, understand complex logic, and boost productivity without breaking your flow.

This blog walks you through that setup and helps you experience how seamless and powerful SAP development becomes when GenAI is built directly into your everyday tools.

Before you start using Cline with SAP AI Core, please ensure that your SAP GenAI Hub environment is already set up.
If you have not completed the setup, please follow the links below:

  • SAP AI Core provisioning
    https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-core-genaihub-provisioning..html
  • SAP AI Launchpad provisioning
    https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-launchpad-provisioning..html

Step 1: Install Cline Extensions in VS Code

  1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Go to the Extensions tab

  1. Search for "Cline"
  2. You will see two extensions in the results

  1. Install both: Cline & Cline for SAP AI Core

After installation, close and reopen VS Code.

Step 2: Create or Use an SAP AI Core Instance

Open your SAP BTP Subaccount.
If you do not already have an SAP AI Core instance, create one by following the provisioning guide: https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-core-genaihub-provisioning..html

Once the instance is created:

 

  1. Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Locate your AI Core instance
  3. Create a Service Key
  4. You will use the details from this service key to configure Cline

Example service key:

Step 3: Configure Cline With SAP AI Core Credentials

In Cline,
API Provider: SAP AI Core
AI Core Client Id: <take the info from key : clientid>
AI Core Client Secret: < take the info from key : clientsecret>
AI Core Base URL: <take the info from key : AI_API_URL>
AI Core Auth URL: <take the info from key : url>
AI Core Resource Group: <by default, group will be default. If you have any specific group please specify the name>
Orchestration Mode: If you have an extended service plan, the “Orchestration Mode” checkbox will automatically appear
       Note: If Orchestration Mode is checked, you instantly get access to all models via the Harmonized API with no deployments, else you must deploy and manage each model separately.
Model:  Choose desired model from the “dropdown”

Step 4: Verify That Cline Is Working

  1. Click the Cline icon in the VS Code sidebar
  2. Type a test prompt, such as: “Write a 1-line JavaScript function.”
  3. Press Enter

You should now see the response.
🎉 Cline is successfully connected to SAP AI Core!



By integrating Cline with SAP AI Core, you now have a practical and efficient way to use SAP’s GenAI capabilities directly within your development workflow. This setup allows you to generate CAP service logic, refine CDS models, create UI5 components, validate integration flows, and troubleshoot code without jumping between multiple tools.

Using your AI Core service key, endpoints, and resource group, Cline securely connects to SAP’s GenAI Hub and gives you quick access to both Harmonized and model-specific APIs. This ensures that your prompts return consistent, SAP-relevant outputs that fit naturally into your existing project structure.

With everything configured and your first prompt tested, you can now explore different models, try more advanced scenarios, and make AI a reliable part of your daily SAP development routine. This simple addition can make your workflow faster, cleaner, and much more enjoyable.

E2E information available in the attached blog - https://docs.cline.bot/provider-config/sap-aicore

entry.title: Accelerating SAPUI5 Migration to BAS: A Practical Framework
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP has transitioned its development paradigm from SAP Web IDE to SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), pushing developers to migrate from legacy UI5 to Fiori applications to the new environment. This paper outlines a structured and scalable methodology to migrate SAP UI5 applications from WEB IDE to BAS, focusing on connectivity, tooling and configuration challenges.

Methodology:

  1. Log in to your BTP Account. Go into your space. Go to the Service Marketplace. Search for Business Application Studio. Open Business Application Studio Application.

 

Note: Pre-Requisite - Cloud Connector and Destination should be set up for connectivity with the S4.

2. Click on Create Dev Space. Give some Dev Space Name—select the SAP Fiori on the left. On the right side, under Additional SAP Extensions select HTML5 Application Template, Launchpad Module, Basic UI5 Template. Click on Create Dev Space.

3. Dev Space is created as shown below. It will take a few minutes to start. Once it's started, click on the Dev space. Here, I have named it “DemoMigration”. It will open the Development workspace. Bookmark this URL for future use and easy navigation to Workspace.

4. For the first time, click on the menu on the top left and select the open folder. Search for Projects Folder. Click on OK.

5. Click on Import on your Get Started page. It will prompt to start the migration. Select migration option. Below screen will appear.  

 

6. Select Destination and Hostname and Client Details.

Conclusion:

Migrating SAP UI5 applications from SAP Web IDE to SAP Business Application Studio is more than a technical upgrade. It represents a strategic change towards a cloud-native and future ready development environment aligned with that of SAP’s roadmap. This blog outlined a well-structured migration methodology, which includes prerequisite connectivity set up, dev space configuration, project import and environment preparation within BAS. By following these steps, organizations can ensure a smooth transition while minimizing disruption to existing Fiori applications. This structured approach simplifies migration from WEB IDE to BAS. It supports scalable and cloud-aligned SAPUI5 development for public institutions and enterprises.

.

entry.title: Part 1: Creating and Deploying a SAP CAP Application with SAP HANA on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry)
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the First Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone

 

Introduction

SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is often demonstrated through local examples, but its real value becomes clear when the application is deployed to SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and backed by a managed database.

In this blog, we will go through creating a basic SAP CAP application using the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) CAP template, binding it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deploying it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime.

The goal here is not to build a complex application, but to establish a clean, working reference point. I start with a minimal CAP project to keep the focus on deployment rather than domain complexity.

This blog serves as a baseline for a series where the same CAP application will later be deployed using different databases and runtimes. The focus here is not application complexity, but establishing a clean, working reference point.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • Active Hana Cloud Instance
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

 

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a CAP Project from Template given in the BAS:

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-hana-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: SAP HANA Cloud
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

 

CAP Project creation wizard with SAP HANA selected

 

This will create a CAP project for you with mta.yaml automatically updated. The generated project follows standard CAP structure:

  • app –  UI
  • db – Schema and data files
  • srv – Service Definition

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Development:

Follow the following steps:

  • Define a Simple Data Model:

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;
entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

               

  • Expose the Model via CAP Service:

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

  • Add Data:

Run the following in the project terminal:

cds add data --records 5

 

  • Run the Application locally:

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build
cds deploy

 

  • Add a Managed Approuter:
  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

 

This is important to view the data once deployed.

 

Prepare the Project for Deployment:

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

 

Build the CAP Project:

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application:

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry deployment

 

During deployment:

  • HDI Container is created
  • Database schema is deployed
  • CAP Service is bound to the database automatically

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application:

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

With that, we have successfully created a minimal SAP CAP application, connected it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime. While the application itself is intentionally simple, the deployment pipeline we established here — from schema definition to MTA build to Cloud Foundry deployment — reflects a production-ready workflow that scales well as complexity grows.

The key takeaways from this exercise are that the BAS CAP template significantly reduces boilerplate by auto-generating the mta.yaml with the necessary modules and bindings, the HDI container handles schema deployment seamlessly, and CAP's convention-over-configuration approach means your OData service is ready without any manual REST wiring.

This baseline will serve as the foundation for the rest of this series, where we will explore deploying the same application with different databases, runtimes, and configurations — making it easier to understand the trade-offs of each approach. Stay tuned for the next part!

 

Next Steps

In the upcoming blogs, we will focus on creating the same application using a PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option.

entry.title: Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean Core, BTP Driven Strategy for ModernUtilities
entry.content:
Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean‑Core, BTP‑Driven Strategy for Modern Utilities

Executive Summary

If you’ve spent any time around utility billing operations, you know mass reversals aren’t rare — they’re part of the job. Sometimes it’s a rate issue, sometimes a meter read problem, and sometimes it’s just one of those unexpected data surprises that ripple across months of billing, these reversals often span multiple billing cycles and thousands of customers. While the operational process may take days to complete, the customer impact is immediate: as soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on their online portals. This triggers confusion, social media chatter, call‑center spikes, and reputational risk before corrected bills are ready.

What I want to share here is a practical way utilities can handle this without rewriting IS‑U or scrambling teams at 6 AM. We used SAP BTP to create a small visibility layer that sits between IS‑U and the customer portal — and honestly, it solved a problem that has bothered billing teams for years, event‑driven architecture, and lightweight AI to prevent premature credit visibility without modifying SAP IS‑U or customer‑facing systems. The solution introduces a “Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer” that controls what customers see during mass reversals, ensuring operational accuracy without customer panic.

The Practitioner Reality: When a Simple Reversal Becomes a Public Crisis

Every utility billing team has lived through this scenario:

  • A mass correction requires reversing 8–12 months of invoices.
  • The reversal job runs for hours or days.
  • As soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on the portal.
  • Screenshots appear on social media: “Utility X just gave me $900 credit!”
  • Call centers are overwhelmed.
  • Regulators ask questions.
  • The billing team is still in the middle of the correction.

The root cause is simple: SAP IS‑U posts reversals immediately, and customer portals read them immediately. And here’s the part nobody talks about: IS‑U has not built‑in buffer. The moment a reversal posts, the customer sees it. There’s no ‘hold this until we’re done’ button. That’s where the trouble starts. Utilities cannot legally notify customers in advance, and they cannot delay the operational correction. This creates a structural gap between internal billing processes and external customer visibility.

Why This Happens: A Technical Gap in Traditional IS‑U

SAP IS‑U was designed with a real‑time posting model:

  • Reversal document posts →
  • FICA updates →
  • Portal reads →
  • Customer sees credit

There is no concept of:

  • “Temporary suppression”
  • “Billing in progress”
  • “Visibility hold”
  • “Batch publish”

This is not a failure of IS‑U — it’s simply a design that predates modern digital customer expectations.

The Modern Solution: A Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP

Instead of modifying IS‑U or customer portals, utilities can introduce a lightweight, clean‑core extension on SAP BTP that controls visibility, not billing.

This layer sits between:

SAP IS‑U → BTP → Customer Portal / CIS / Mobile App

It does not change billing logic. It does not alter FICA. It does not modify the portal. It simply orchestrates when billing information becomes visible.

Key Capabilities

  • Detect mass reversals in real time
  • Flag accounts as “under correction”
  • Temporarily suppress credit visibility
  • Replace credit display with a neutral message
  • Release final billing once corrections are complete

This simple layer kept customers from seeing half‑baked credits and gave the billing team the breathing room they needed to finish the job properly.

Textual Representation

 

How It Works Using BTP Services

1. SAP Event Mesh — Detect Reversals in Real Time

Event Mesh listens for:

  • Billing document reversals
  • FKK document reversals
  • Mass job triggers

When reversal volume exceeds a threshold (e.g., >500 accounts), it triggers the suppression workflow.

2. SAP BTP Workflow — Manage the Correction Window

Workflow orchestrates:

  • Start of reversal window
  • Suppression period
  • Completion of rebilling
  • Release of final invoice

This creates a controlled, auditable process.

3. SAP BTP Business Rules — Define Visibility Logic

Rules can be configured without coding:

  • “If reversal count > X, suppress visibility for Y hours.”
  • “If account is in correction, show neutral message.”
  • “If rebill fails, extend suppression.”

This keeps the solution clean‑core and upgrade‑safe.

4. SAP HANA Cloud — Temporary Data Store

Stores:

  • Accounts under correction
  • Suppression flags
  • Workflow status
  • Rebill completion indicators

No changes to IS‑U tables.

5. SAP BTP AI — Predict and Prevent Customer Impact

AI can:

  • Identify accounts likely to call
  • Detect social media spikes
  • Predict rebill duration
  • Recommend suppression windows
  • Flag anomalies in reversal patterns

This turns a reactive process into a proactive one.

What the Customer Sees Instead

Instead of a large credit, the portal displays:

“Your billing is being updated. Your final balance will be available shortly.”

This avoids:

  • Panic
  • Misinterpretation
  • Social media escalation
  • Call center overload

Clean‑Core Alignment

This approach is fully clean‑core because:

  • No IS‑U modifications
  • No FICA changes
  • No portal rewrites
  • No custom Z‑tables in SAP
  • All logic runs on BTP
  • Fully upgrade‑safe
  • Works with ECC and S/4HANA

This is exactly what SAP means by “extensibility without modification.”

Practitioner Example

In one of the utilities I worked with, we had to reverse nearly 10 months of bills for over 180,000 customers. Within 45 minutes, screenshots of huge credits were all over social media. The call center was drowning. Regulators were pinging leadership. It was chaos — and all because the portal updated too early.

After implementing a BTP‑based visibility layer:

  • Mass reversals ran silently
  • No customer saw temporary credits
  • No social media chatter
  • No call center spike
  • Corrected bills were published cleanly

The billing team completed the same process with zero customer disruption.

Conclusion

Mass invoice reversals will always be part of utility operations. But customer visibility does not need to be immediate or uncontrolled. By introducing a Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP — powered by Event Mesh, Workflow, Business Rules, HANA Cloud, and AI — utilities can protect customer experience, reduce operational risk, and maintain clean‑core principles.

This is modernization where it matters: not in rewriting billing logic, but in orchestrating how and when customers see the results.

entry.title: Part 2: Creating and Deploying a CAP Application with PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the Second Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone.

 

Introduction

In the Part 1 of this series, a CAP application has been created and deployed to SAP BTP using SAP HANA Cloud. In the current blog, I will focus on creating and deploying a CAP application to BTP using the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option provided by SAP. This approach demonstrates a PostgreSQL-first workflow and focuses on the specific configuration required to use PostgreSQL as the persistence layer.

 

Why PostgreSQL on SAP BTP

You might have a question, “If a CAP application can be deployed with SAP HANA Cloud, then why go with an alternative Database?”

While the SAP HANA Cloud is still the best choice for enterprise applications, CAP also allows seamless integration with alternative databases with minimal changes.

PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP is useful in scenarios such as:

  • Cost-sensitive environments
  • Open-source aligned architectures
  • Hybrid cloud landscapes

From the CAP application perspective, the CDS model and service definitions remain unchanged.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option service subcription
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a New CAP Project in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-postgres-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: PostgreSQL
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

CAP project creation wizard

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Define the Data Model

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;

entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

 

Expose the Model via a CAP Service

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

Add Data

Run the following in BAS Terminal

cds add data –records 5

 

Run the Application locally

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build

cds deploy

 

Add a Managed Approuter

  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

This is important to view the data once deployed.

Prepare the Project for Deployment

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

Though everything is ready, you need to make a small change for Postgres Deployment. Make the following change:

  • In MTA, go to resources
  • Navigate to <app-name>-postgres
  • Change the service-plan from development to trial

 

mta.yaml visible in the BAS workspace

 

Note : Do this only if you are using a Trial Account. If you are in an enterprise account, please go to your Instances and Subscription and copy the service-plan from PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option and look for the plan under Service Plan.

 

Configure CAP to Use PostgreSQL

Navigate into the project folder and install the PostgreSQL driver:

cds add postgres

Note: This step is not important as BAS Template must have already added this. This could be used while creating the CAP application without the Template wizard.

 

Build the CAP Project

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-postgres-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry Deployment

 

During deployment

  • PostgreSQL credentials are injected automatically via service binding
  • CAP initializes the database schema
  • The application starts without additional configuration

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

Deploying an SAP CAP application with the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP highlights CAP’s database-agnostic architecture. The CDS models and service logic remain unchanged, while PostgreSQL is integrated seamlessly through minimal configuration and service binding.

This approach provides flexibility for cost-sensitive and open-source aligned environments while still leveraging SAP BTP’s enterprise capabilities such as automated schema deployment, secure credential management, and simplified lifecycle handling through Cloud Foundry and MTA deployment.

Overall, PostgreSQL on SAP BTP enables developers to build scalable, cloud-native CAP applications with greater persistence flexibility, while retaining the option to transition to SAP HANA Cloud in the future without significant code changes.

 

Next Steps

In the next part of this series, the deployed CAP application will be integrated with SAP Build Work Zone, demonstrating how backend services can be exposed through a secure, user-friendly interface to deliver a complete end-to-end enterprise application experience.
